speaker: interviewer
text: From New York?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please. Yeah. Continue with New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. We lived in New York on the lower east side like I said. My husband didn't have a job. It was very difficult to find anything. He didn't have a trade that he could show that he was educated. We didn't have that, because we didn't have too much education. We never went to college or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived with other people. But the people were very nice to us. And she-- she had a pity on those that we didn't have nothing. And she went to a neighbor who had a candy store. And she said, I have people living there. Maybe you know where he can go to work? And this man worked in the shoe factory, where my husband was ordering the shoes. And he said, I give you the address. Maybe you can contact him? And that's how my husband came into the shoe factory and worked there for 10 years. Did-- did you do anything? I know that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you've been a bookkeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't do bookkeeping. I-- when my boy was a little bit bigger that he went to kindergarten, I had in the same building that we lived was a candy store. I went downstairs and I always talked with the man. And I said if he would need somebody, I would be glad to do anything to make some money, because we needed it. So he said to me, fine, if you want to work, I hire you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took my kid to school. And in the hours that he was in school, I worked in the candy store. And after that, I had him playing in front of the house. I knew all the neighbors very well and they liked me. And so they watched my-- my Barry. That's how I started working. When we went away, we saved everything we could. We never spent a penny. Because we knew that that was not the solution, that we wanted to start something on our own. How long can he work in the factory? He worked there on a-- on piece work. He made 2,000 pieces a day sewed on-- on the machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew that that cannot be forever. There was no air condition, no fans, nothing in the factory. When he used to come home, I used to wring out his-- his clothing from the sweat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So little by little, we saved everything we had. We bought us furniture. And we bought a car. And we wanted to go into some business, but we had not enough money to buy some business. And we didn't know what-- we had no trade, so we didn't know what to do. So my husband met a coworker, and he went to Denver. And he was praising Denver, how beautiful it was. So my husband said, you know what? Let's look. Let's go and look.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we came to Denver. We went to look. We liked it. We didn't know what we were going to do there. But we rented an house. We left everything that we had in the car. And we went back to Denver. We went back on a Monday, and we came back Friday. And we have sold everything, no matter what we got for it. We took a U-Haul and we moved to Denver. And in Denver, we rented a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of store?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a shoe store and a shoe repair combination. I used to work with him together. I learned how to fix. I used to stay in the store to sell. And I learned how to help him. And I used to go every day to the store and help out. In the meantime, my son grew up. He was bar mitzvah in Denver. And he helped, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years did you stay in Denver for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years. My husband didn't agree with the altitude. He couldn't stand the altitude, and we wanted a change. We didn't want to go back to New York. So we said, then, there is nothing left. We're going to go to California.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we picked up and we went to San Jose, because everybody told us that Los Angeles is terrible. The smog and this. So we didn't go to it. We went up north. So we went to San Jose.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if my husband told you the story that we met there some people. And they promised us the year before we were here to see if we can get settled. And they said we could work by Levi Strauss. When we came back the following year, Levi Strauss was not hiring. He was firing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we couldn't get a job there. So I went out in the evening. I was babysitting in a bowling alley-- the children. I did that at night. In the daytime, I was home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband met another refugee who was a carpenter. And he took him in to become a carpenter. We-- we didn't like it. We didn't want to stay there. And we came-- we went to-- to Los Angeles. We liked it very much because it was Jewish. And in San Jose, we lived in a completely non-Jewish area, because there isn't hardly any Jewish area. And we saw Fairfax and we liked it, because we grew up in a similar neighborhood like that as a child. So we went back. We took a big U-Haul. We packed everything up, and we came to Los Angeles. And my son was very happy. He went to Fairfax High School, and he said, ma, you took me from one extreme to another. Because in Cupertino, where we lived, there were only two Jews in high school in the 2,000 kids. So I didn't like this idea there at all, and we came to Fairfax. And here, he had all Jewish friends. He went to Fairfax High School. And he later went to college. And that's how he-- we stood here. And I knew that I wanted to do something. I didn't want to go work in a factory. So I went to Fairfax High School, and I applied for bookkeeping. And I went there in the evening. I took my husband [INAUDIBLE] along. He didn't care for it too much, but I loved it and became very good at it. And they either offered me a job if I wanted to go and teach and things like that. I said no. I went and I graduated. And I went to the Jewish organization here. And I applied for a bookkeeping job. And somebody hired me. It was on Western Avenue. That was my first job. And I was an assistant to the man as a bookkeeper. I stood there a year, but I had no insurance. I had nothing there. So I said, I want to get out and get something. And I applied to-- to an agent. And that became accounts payable for Revlon Cosmetic. And then, later on, Revlon moved away and I became to another company who was Helene-- associated with Helene Curtis. I stood there, and I worked for them. And later on, I lost this job too, because this company moved to the Valley and I couldn't go there. I had no transportation. But in the meantime, my son went into business with my husband and I worked with them. And I still do the books for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what your Jewish life is like now.

speaker: interviewee
text: We go to the high holidays to the temple. We have a Jewish daughter-in-law, thank God. We have two grandchildren. Both of them went to [INAUDIBLE] and were bar mitzvah there in the temple. My older grandson is a straight A student. He is in Berkeley. This is his second year. [INAUDIBLE] my little one goes to high school, is a very good student. And we hope that he can go to go to a good college, too. My grandson wants to become, I think, a lawyer. So he goes now to Berkeley, and then we will see to which law school he gets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any last things that you would like to say for-- for the viewers and for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a store that we hire mostly Mexican people, because we are in a Mexican neighborhood. We try to be good to them and help them as much as they can, because we know how it is to be in a foreign country by-- by yourself. We have some workers with us for many years. I have one for 15 years that works for us that came to me as a child and is now married and has children. And we have most of the workers for a long time. We try to be good and treat them fair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you want to say about your-- your Holocaust experiences for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only tell you that we were living in Vienna, Austria. It was always antisemitic. But we never thought of something like that could happen. We had no idea what was going on. Not in the-- in our dreams could we imagine gas chamber and Auschwitz or something like that. We couldn't believe it. We went back after the war. We saw Dachau. We saw the gas chamber, and I broke down. I couldn't believe that something like that could happen. I went to Israel the first time. We were in the kibbutz. We met a German woman, not Jewish. She was sitting with us on the table. She lived near Dachau and she told me that she didn't know. I started talking with her. She started crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, how could you not know? You could have smelled it. And she said, I didn't know. So I-- I-- I couldn't take it, you know, because I went against it. She started crying. I said, I don't care. You must have smelt what was coming out of the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am sorry to say the new generation has nothing to do with it, but I cannot forget. I cannot forget my parents. I cannot forget it happened. Many time I have dreams. It comes back to me many, many times. That I run and I-- I am chased. I'm running. As long as I live, I will always have that in my memory. I cannot forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell it to my grandchildren. And they should know what's going on. My grandchildren both know. And I tell them, I hope to God that it never happened, but I didn't expect that it happened to us and it could happen again. I hope to God that I'm wrong, because I don't want it should happened again. But like we didn't expect anything, you don't know what's going to happen in-- in the generations to come. I hope it never going to happen no more. But like I say, we-- we had no idea-- no idea that something like that could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my mother. She was the best mother in the world, and I will never forget her. Her name was Mechle [NON-ENGLISH] married name Leiter Marasch. It was in Vienna in 1939 when the photo was taken, before she was taken away. After that, I never saw her no more. This was my father. At the time, he had cancer. Already you can see how skinny he is. He was a very heavy man before. His name was Berl Leiter Marasch. And the picture was taken in 1939 just before he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria. He is buried in Vienna. I made the grave for him. I made the stone for him. And I put my mother's name and my brother's name on the same stone, so we should have a memory. He is buried in Vienna in the [NON-ENGLISH] in the Jewish cemetery. This is my sister, my only sister that I ever had. Her name was Peppi. She was married to Alfred Rein. And that was also one of the latest pictures I ever had for her. She died on cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and-- and where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken I think in Arizona, in Tempe, Arizona, where she moved from New York. And she came later to Los Angeles and died here on brain cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the year. I couldn't-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Roughly? How many years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that be around 1965?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. 30 years, something like that, ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 30 Year-- '65. '60, '65, I don't know exactly which year it was. This is my wedding picture. It was taken on 11 September 1940. At the time, Hitler was in Amsterdam already. We couldn't make too big of a wedding anymore. So we had the family only, and very few friends. The first one from left is my brother-in-law Paul. Next to is my father-in-law, [? Aaron ?] David. All of them are Seemans. Then comes my sister-in-law, also Seeman. Then, comes my husband and me. Next to it is a good friend of my husband. Her name was [? Frieda ?] [? Feldstein. ?] Then, was my brother [? Izzy ?] and his wife [? Yeti. ?] None of these people-- not my sister-in-law, not my brother, not Mrs. [? Felstein, ?] not my father-in-law-- none of them survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Paul did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul? Yeah. Paul remained and his wife, because he was a mixed marriage. She was not Jewish. So he was safe to her, otherwise. He was in the-- in camp. He was taken away, but he came back. But otherwise, none of them survived. This is our wedding picture. We were married in September 11, 1940. We were very young. I was 20 years. My husband was 30 years. And we are married by now 55 years. His name is Abraham Seeman. My name was Charlotte Marasch Leiter Seeman. It was taken in Amsterdam. And the date of the photo, like I say, was September 11, 1940. This paper was given to us by the underground, when we were moving from one place another. There was no Jews no more there. So we were given Christian paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so called, we came from out of town from the ocean where we were bombed. My husband's name was [? Holtman ?] [? Wilhelm ?] Abraham. And my name was Reussink Emilie Charlotte. You can see my picture on it, and my name was Reussink Emilie Charlotte. You want more?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken after the war, after five years of Hitler. It-- I think it was in 1945. And I was 25. My husband was 35. His name is Abraham Seeman. My name is Charlotte Seeman. It was taken in Amsterdam, and I think it was right after the war. We didn't have no child yet. It was in 1945. This is my darling son, Barry. It was taken when he was six weeks old, when we had to go to the American Council to make pictures for the passport. This was his passport picture, because he couldn't sit yet. He was born September 16, 1948, in Amsterdam. His name is Barry Seeman. And this was his first picture that they took. He was six weeks old. This is a picture that we have taken by my youngest-- younger son-- grandson's bar mitzvah. His name is Danny Aron Seeman. This picture was taken on his 13th birthday, when he was just bar mitzvah. From left, I will start. It's my husband Abraham. Next to him is Lucie Kloek. That's the lady that saved our life. Next to him-- next to her is Michael Seeman, my older grandson. Then comes my daughter-in-law, [? Ruthie ?] Emily Seeman. Then comes my son, Barry Seeman. And next to them is the young lady, is the daughter from Lucie, the younger daughter for Lucie, and her little boy. His name is [INAUDIBLE] and her-- her name is [? Lilah. ?] [? Lilah ?] Kloek. And [INAUDIBLE]. In front of it is myself, Charlotte Seeman, and my grandson, Danny. This-- we brought them all from Amsterdam-- Lucie and her daughter, and the little baby. He was-- they were honored in the temple, one of the few people that ever carried the Torah was Lucie in the temple in [INAUDIBLE]. We had them before that. We went to Israel. We took Lucie and her older daughter to Jerusalem and we had them honored in [INAUDIBLE]. There was a tree put down for her and her husband in the righteous-- for the righteous Gentile. Also, a big block was made there for the people that came from out-- out of-- that saved Jewish people. We had big honors they are given. We were in [INAUDIBLE]. The head cantor had a ceremony, because we were 22 Gentiles that came from-- from Russia, from the Ukraine. And Lucie was amongst the one that came from Holland. She had big honor given from the mayor of the city for Jerusalem. From-- from-- from Tel Aviv, we were on television. We were on radio. We were taken around all over Israel to show her what Israel had built up and how-- how it looks today. We were there two weeks, and we had a wonderful tour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her-- her name? The-- I talked you-- I told you the-- all the names. No, no. Yes. I know you told me all the names. Where-- where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken by the bar mitzvah in Los Angeles in the Ventura Club, where we had his-- in the evening the party.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what the date was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In September, 19-- four-- three years ago, in September 1992. That was his bar mitzvah picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Charlotte, can you please tell us who this man is sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely husband Abraham Seeman, my husband for 55 years. And we have this week our anniversary. On September 11, 1995, we will have our 55 anniversary. We were married in 1940 in Amsterdam, Holland. I should kiss him. Ah. [NON-ENGLISH]. No. Not right. But you should only be well. I should have you many more years.
